text:
Situation A. More Containers

The challenge of designing inexpensive packaging also applies to other products. Consider the following requests for packaging cube-shaped boxes.

For each request, do the following:

  1. Find the dimensions of the rectangular prisms (boxes) that require the least packaging material. Explain your strategy.
  2. Enter the data from the requests in a table. Look for patterns among the dimensions of the boxes. What type of box has the least surface area for a fixed volume?
  3. Suppose you want to package 500 cubic centimeters of pasta in a cube-shaped box. What are the dimensions of the cube with the least surface area.
The cube-shaped boxes need the least packaging material. Some of the unit cubes or their faces are "hidden" inside and do not add to the surface area.

3. Since 8 x 8 x 8 = 512 and 7 x 7 x 7 = 343, we can guess and check fractions between 7 and 8.

7.8 x 7.8 x 7.8 = 454.56

7.9 x 7.9 x 7.9 = 493.04

7.95 x 7.95 x 7.95 = 502.46

7.94 x 7.94 x 7. 94 = 500.57

So 7.94 cm seems to be a good estimate for a cube that holds 500 cubic centimeters. This has a surface area of about 378.3 Square centimeters.
Note: Students are developing an intuitive sense of cube root. Students will revisit the idea of a cube root is in Grade 8.

Situation B. Students’ Representations of Volume and Surface Area

Students in Ms. Stewart’s class notice patterns in their tables for volume and surface area. They wrote equations to represent these patterns for rectangular prisms.

  • V is the volume
  • l is the length
  • w is the width
  • h is the height
  • S is the surface area
1. Examine each student's thinking, and explain why it does or does not make sense.

Malek's Pattern

I noticed that the volume V of a rectangular prism is l • w • h.
Students should recognize that l x w is the base or one layer and then multiplying by h is multiplying by the number of layers in the prism. V = B • h or V = l • w • h

Nia's Pattern

I noticed that the volume V of a rectangular prism is area of the base x h.

Students should recognize that the area of the base is the same as l x w or finding how many are in one layer and then by multiplying by h, that multiplies it by the numbers of layers in the prism. V = B • h or V = l • w • h

Kavya's Pattern

I noticed that the surface area is the sum of the area of the sides of the prisms. So, I wrote S = 2(lw + lh + hw).
Students should recognize that when they apply the Distributive Property, they would have the area all 6 faces of the prism.
2. Explain why it might be useful to use expression and equations to represent volume and surface area of a rectangular prism.
Expressions and equations are an efficient way to express a pattern or generalization. They can be thought of as short ways of making statements about relationships.
